content.text: Sessions in the OpenAI Agents SDK
manages multi-turn conversation history so agents remember what was said earlier. Without sessions, every call to
is stateless — you would have to manually pass the prior conversation as
. Sessions automate this bookkeeping, making it easy to build long-running chatbots, assistants, and interactive workflows.
A session is identified by a
string. Any run associated with that ID reads the stored history before calling the model and appends the new messages after the run completes.

attrs.alt: OpenAI Agents SDK session conversation management diagram

content.text: Sessions implement a simple async interface:
returns the stored messages for a session, and
appends new messages after a run. The SDK ships two implementations.
Persistence across restarts, multi-user apps with file-based storage
The simplest session keeps history in memory. It is cleared when the process restarts.

import asyncio
from agents import Agent, Runner
from agents.memory import SimpleInMemorySession

agent = Agent(name="ChatBot", instructions="Be helpful and concise.")

session = SimpleInMemorySession()

async def chat(user_message: str) -> str:
    result = await Runner.run(agent, user_message, session=session)
    return result.final_output

async def main():
    print(await chat("My name is Alice."))    # "Nice to meet you, Alice!"
    print(await chat("What is my name?"))     # "Your name is Alice."
    print(await chat("How many turns so far?"))  # "We've had 3 turns."

asyncio.run(main())
call is all that is required. The SDK reads the prior messages from the session before calling the model and appends the new turn's messages after.
For persistent memory that survives process restarts, use
. It stores history in a local SQLite file, keyed by session ID.

import asyncio
from agents import Agent, Runner
from agents.memory import SQLiteSession

agent = Agent(name="PersistentBot", instructions="Be helpful.")

async def main():
    # Re-running this script continues the same conversation
    session = SQLiteSession(
        session_id="user-42-support-chat",
        db_path="./chat_history.db",   # created automatically if absent
    )

    result = await Runner.run(agent, "What did I ask last time?", session=session)
    print(result.final_output)

asyncio.run(main())
creates the database file and table automatically on first use. The
is the primary key — different IDs give independent conversation histories in the same file.
Create one session object per user. Each user gets an isolated conversation history even when agents are shared module-level singletons.

import asyncio
from agents import Agent, Runner
from agents.memory import SQLiteSession

_agent = Agent(name="Support", instructions="Help users with their accounts.")

async def handle_request(user_id: str, message: str) -> str:
    session = SQLiteSession(
        session_id=f"user-{user_id}",
        db_path="./sessions.db",
    )
    result = await Runner.run(_agent, message, session=session)
    return result.final_output

async def main():
    # Alice and Bob have independent conversation histories
    print(await handle_request("alice", "My plan expires tomorrow."))
    print(await handle_request("bob", "I need a refund."))
    print(await handle_request("alice", "What did I just tell you?"))

asyncio.run(main())
Read the current session history for debugging or building context-aware UIs.

import asyncio
from agents.memory import SQLiteSession

async def main():
    session = SQLiteSession("user-42-support-chat", "./chat_history.db")
    items = await session.get_items()

    for item in items:
        role = item.get("role", "unknown")
        content = item.get("content", "")
        if isinstance(content, list):
            # multi-part content (text + tool calls)
            text_parts = [c.get("text", "") for c in content if isinstance(c, dict)]
            content = " ".join(text_parts)
        print(f"[{role}]: {content[:80]}")

asyncio.run(main())
To reset a conversation — for example when a user starts a new chat thread — clear the session. The session object remains usable; subsequent runs start with an empty history.

import asyncio
from agents.memory import SQLiteSession

async def main():
    session = SQLiteSession("user-42-support-chat", "./chat_history.db")
    await session.delete()   # wipes all messages for this session_id

asyncio.run(main())
removes the history for this specific
from the database. Other sessions stored in the same file are unaffected.
protocol to store history in Redis, PostgreSQL, DynamoDB, or any other backend.

from typing import Any
from agents.memory import Session

class RedisSession(Session):
    def __init__(self, session_id: str, redis_client):
        self.session_id = session_id
        self.redis = redis_client
        self._key = f"agent_session:{session_id}"

    async def get_items(self) -> list[dict[str, Any]]:
        import json
        raw = await self.redis.get(self._key)
        return json.loads(raw) if raw else []

    async def add_items(self, items: list[dict[str, Any]]) -> None:
        import json
        current = await self.get_items()
        current.extend(items)
        await self.redis.set(self._key, json.dumps(current))
Pass an instance of your custom backend to
chaining solve the same problem. Sessions automate the bookkeeping; manual chaining gives more control.
You call to_input_list() and pass it on every turn
Not built in — implement in a custom backend
You control which messages to pass
You control which history goes to which agent
Pipelines and workflows with custom history logic
A common production pattern uses a session per HTTP request-body conversation ID, backed by SQLite or a remote store.

from fastapi import FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel
from agents import Agent, Runner
from agents.memory import SQLiteSession

app = FastAPI()
_agent = Agent(name="AssistantBot", instructions="Help customers.")

class ChatRequest(BaseModel):
    session_id: str
    message: str

@app.post("/chat")
async def chat(req: ChatRequest) -> dict:
    session = SQLiteSession(req.session_id, "./sessions.db")
    result = await Runner.run(_agent, req.message, session=session)
    return {"reply": result.final_output}
Understanding the exact steps the SDK performs during a session-aware run clarifies what you can customise.
The input (user message) is appended to the history to form the full message list.
The model is called with the full message list.
Tool calls and their results are processed (multiple model calls may occur).
The final assistant message is produced.
messages from the current run, not the full conversation. This is efficient for large histories — it does not re-write data that is already stored.
Each session object is tied to one
. Concurrent access to the same session from multiple async tasks is possible but must be handled carefully.

import asyncio
from agents import Agent, Runner
from agents.memory import SQLiteSession

agent = Agent(name="Bot", instructions="Help users.")

async def handle(user_id: str, message: str) -> str:
    # Each user gets their own session — no contention
    session = SQLiteSession(f"user-{user_id}", "./sessions.db")
    result = await Runner.run(agent, message, session=session)
    return result.final_output

async def main():
    # These run concurrently but use different session_ids — safe
    results = await asyncio.gather(
        handle("alice", "Hello!"),
        handle("bob", "Hi there!"),
    )
    print(results)

asyncio.run(main())
Risky: same session_id from concurrent tasks

# This pattern is unsafe — two concurrent tasks may interleave writes
# to the same session and corrupt the conversation order.

session = SQLiteSession("shared-session", "./sessions.db")

# DON'T do this:
results = await asyncio.gather(
    Runner.run(agent, "Message A", session=session),
    Runner.run(agent, "Message B", session=session),
)
If you need concurrent access to one conversation, use an asyncio lock to serialise runs sharing the same session:

import asyncio
from agents import Agent, Runner
from agents.memory import SQLiteSession

_locks: dict[str, asyncio.Lock] = {}

def get_lock(session_id: str) -> asyncio.Lock:
    if session_id not in _locks:
        _locks[session_id] = asyncio.Lock()
    return _locks[session_id]

async def safe_run(agent, message: str, session_id: str) -> str:
    session = SQLiteSession(session_id, "./sessions.db")
    async with get_lock(session_id):
        result = await Runner.run(agent, message, session=session)
    return result.final_output

Long-running sessions accumulate many messages. At some point, the history grows large enough to slow model calls and increase token costs. Prune history before it is passed to the model by wrapping the session.

from typing import Any
from agents.memory import Session

class WindowedSession(Session):
    """Keep only the last N messages."""

    def __init__(self, inner: Session, max_messages: int = 20):
        self._inner = inner
        self._max = max_messages

    async def get_items(self) -> list[dict[str, Any]]:
        all_items = await self._inner.get_items()
        return all_items[-self._max:]  # keep only the most recent N messages

    async def add_items(self, items: list[dict[str, Any]]) -> None:
        await self._inner.add_items(items)

# Usage
from agents.memory import SQLiteSession

base_session = SQLiteSession("user-42", "./sessions.db")
windowed_session = WindowedSession(base_session, max_messages=30)

result = await Runner.run(agent, user_message, session=windowed_session)
A more sophisticated pruning strategy summarises older messages and prepends the summary as a system message. This preserves context without passing the full history.

import asyncio
from agents import Agent, Runner
from agents.memory import Session, SQLiteSession
from typing import Any

summariser = Agent(
    name="Summariser",
    instructions=(
        "You are a conversation summariser. "
        "Summarise the conversation below in 3-5 bullet points. "
        "Preserve key facts, decisions, and user preferences."
    ),
)

class SummarisedSession(Session):
    def __init__(self, inner: Session, threshold: int = 40):
        self._inner = inner
        self._threshold = threshold

    async def get_items(self) -> list[dict[str, Any]]:
        items = await self._inner.get_items()
        if len(items) <= self._threshold:
            return items

        # Summarise all but the last 10 messages
        old = items[:-10]
        recent = items[-10:]

        summary_text = "
".join(
            f"{m['role']}: {m.get('content','')}" for m in old
        )
        result = await Runner.run(summariser, summary_text)
        summary_message = {
            "role": "system",
            "content": f"[Conversation summary]
{result.final_output}",
        }
        return [summary_message] + recent

    async def add_items(self, items: list[dict[str, Any]]) -> None:
        await self._inner.add_items(items)

When an agent uses handoffs, the session stores the full interleaved history from all agents in the chain. The target agent automatically receives the history from prior agents, including any messages produced by the originating agent.

import asyncio
from agents import Agent, Runner
from agents.memory import SimpleInMemorySession

billing = Agent(name="Billing", instructions="Handle billing issues.")
triage = Agent(
    name="Triage",
    instructions="Route billing questions to Billing.",
    handoffs=[billing],
)

session = SimpleInMemorySession()

async def main():
    # Turn 1: triage handles a general question
    result = await Runner.run(triage, "What payment methods do you accept?", session=session)
    print(result.last_agent.name, "—", result.final_output)

    # Turn 2: billing agent picks up context from Turn 1
    result = await Runner.run(triage, "I was charged twice this month.", session=session)
    print(result.last_agent.name, "—", result.final_output)

    # The session contains messages from both agents in chronological order
    items = await session.get_items()
    print(f"Total messages stored: {len(items)}")

asyncio.run(main())

import asyncio
from agents.memory import SQLiteSession

async def debug_session(session_id: str, db_path: str) -> None:
    session = SQLiteSession(session_id, db_path)
    items = await session.get_items()
    print(f"Session '{session_id}': {len(items)} messages")
    for i, item in enumerate(items):
        role = item.get("role", "?")
        raw_content = item.get("content", "")
        if isinstance(raw_content, list):
            content = " | ".join(
                c.get("text", str(c)) for c in raw_content
                if isinstance(c, dict)
            )
        else:
            content = str(raw_content)
        print(f"  [{i}] {role}: {content[:100]}")

asyncio.run(debug_session("user-42-support-chat", "./sessions.db"))

items = await session.get_items()
user_turns = sum(1 for m in items if m.get("role") == "user")
assistant_turns = sum(1 for m in items if m.get("role") == "assistant")
tool_results = sum(1 for m in items if m.get("role") == "tool")
print(f"user={user_turns}, assistant={assistant_turns}, tool_results={tool_results}")
— store all users' sessions in a single SQLite file, distinguished by
. This is simpler to manage than one file per user.
field in a custom backend and prune sessions older than N days from a nightly job to control storage growth.
— store sessions in an immutable append-only log (e.g. S3) for compliance; your custom backend reads from a Redis cache and writes to both.
to a file for offline analysis or to replay a conversation against a different model or agent configuration.
Sessions automatically persist and replay conversation history across multiple
Create one session per user or conversation thread by using a unique
protocol to plug in Redis, PostgreSQL, or any other backend
Use sessions for stateful chatbots; use manual
when you need fine-grained control over which history is passed to which agent
